name: Graph Standard Format
description: GSF is a small set of rules for visualizing relationships. It is the grammar a human or a machine follows so they do not have to decide how to show a relationship graph. GSF is the rules, not the data: a dataset conforms to GSF; a renderer applies GSF to that dataset to produce the picture. Like CSF (its sibling for funds-flow sequence diagrams), GSF keeps the grammar small so the catalog can stay large.

what_gsf_is.gsf_is_the_rules: GSF is a ruleset, not a dataset. This file is never visualized. It defines how any conforming dataset should be drawn.

three_things.rules: GSF - the grammar, primitives, encoding, and density behavior in this file.
three_things.dataset: A {view, nodes, links} instance that conforms to GSF. This is what gets visualized.
three_things.renderer: Takes rules + dataset and produces the visualization. d3-force is the canonical renderer.

what_gsf_is.why: So humans and computers do not have to think about how to show relationships. The rules fix the mapping: a type always means the same shape, a weight always means the same magnitude, a density level always reveals the same amount. The dataset stays dumb; the rules do the work.

primitives.description: GSF has four primitives. Everything a renderer needs is one of these.
primitives.relationship: A link between exactly two entities. Links create the graph; nodes only annotate entities a link already references. Carries source, destination, type, and optionally variables, weight, direction, via.
primitives.type: The qualitative classifier. link.type is what the relationship means (issues, supports, owns); node.type is what the entity is (person, org, concept, system). Drives shape and color.
primitives.variables: The detail bag on a link or node. Holds everything type does not. Splits into value (one quantitative number, carried in weight) and values (qualitative attributes).
primitives.weight: The quantitative magnitude of a link or node (a number). Drives edge width and node size. If absent, node size falls back to degree. The only quantitative encoding input; everything else is qualitative.
primitives.value_vs_values: value (one quantitative number, in weight) drives magnitude; values (qualitative attributes, in variables and type) drive category. This is the whole encoding model.

rules: GSF is the rules for visualizing relationships, not the data and not a storage or analytics model.
A graph is created by links. Every link has source, destination, and type, and connects exactly two entities.
source and destination are entity ids (strings), or endpoint objects {id, value_type, transfer_type} for transfer links.

Field naming: links use source and destination. A d3-force renderer MUST alias destination to d3's target when binding the simulation (d3's link force keys on source/target); target is accepted as an input synonym for destination, which stays canonical. The reference renderer does this in buildG().
Nodes are optional annotations. A node may appear only if its id is a source, destination, or via in at least one link. No orphan nodes.
Direction defaults to directed. Use undirected for symmetric relationships.
When value passes through an exchange or clearing mechanism, name it in link.via; the via entity is a graph member.
Density (level) is disclosure depth; degree is breadth. They are independent.
Force-directed layout is the canonical default. Topology is the signal; do not impose structure the topology does not carry.
Analytics (risk scores, centrality, clustering, alerts) run on the graph; they are not the graph and do not belong in a base dataset.
Every visible node should disclose whether more relationships exist when the source system knows (node.more). Never imply completeness falsely.

encoding.description: The rules that map primitives to pixels, organized as channels so a viewer never decides what a mark means. ACHROMATIC channels carry the story and print in black and white; the CHROMATIC channel (color) is an optional overlay added from Medium. Defaults here; a dataset may override via view.encoding.
encoding.principle: Color is a later, optional layer - never the base. The base reads in B&W: shape for type, line style for relationship class, thickness for magnitude, arrowhead for direction. Anything color adds must already be legible without it.

achromatic_channels.shape: node.type -> shape (default_shapes). Entity category, no color needed.
achromatic_channels.line_style: relationship class -> solid | dashed | dotted (line_styles). The categorical signal for edges in B&W. Mirrors CSF's solid value leg vs dashed data leg.
achromatic_channels.thickness: link.weight -> edge width; node.weight (or degree if absent) -> node size.
achromatic_channels.fill: In Light, all nodes share one neutral fill; shape alone distinguishes type.

default_shapes.description: Canonical shape per entity type. Pairs with color so type survives grayscale, print, and color blindness. A recommendation: a renderer MAY simplify to circles plus color, or a dataset MAY override via view.encoding.shape_by. Edges meet a shape at its bounding radius.

line_styles.description: Relationship class to line style. The achromatic categorical channel for edges. A recommendation; types not listed default to solid.
line_styles.solid: Value or primary structural relationship (transaction, transfers_via, issues, supports, owns).
line_styles.dashed: Data, instruction, or observation that carries no value (instruction, message, observed). CSF's dashed [DATA] leg.
line_styles.dotted: Derived, membership, or abstract link (represents, part_of, member_of, integration).

chromatic_channel.color: node.type -> node color; link.type -> edge color. OPTIONAL overlay, off in Light, on from Medium. Refines, never replaces, the category shape and line style already convey.
chromatic_channel.rule: Color must never be the only signal. Remove color and shape + line style + thickness + arrowhead must still tell the story.

encoding.detail_to_disclosure: variables -> revealed progressively by density level. Light hides them; Heavy shows them all.
encoding.completeness_to_indicator: node.more.exists true -> an expansion indicator. Never imply completeness falsely.

density.description: Light / Medium / Heavy is the disclosure and chroma dial: how much detail and color a renderer reveals. A RULE so the same level renders the same way everywhere. Matches CSF, whose levels also mean amount-of-detail. Breadth (hops from focus) is a separate axis: view.degree.
density.light: The story in black and white. Monochrome, no color. Type from SHAPE; relationship class from LINE STYLE; magnitude from THICKNESS and node size; direction from ARROWHEAD. No per-edge labels. Fast and print-ready, like a CSF sequence diagram.
density.medium: Adds color as an overlay (node and edge color by type), a legend, and variables on hover. more.exists indicators appear.
density.heavy: Adds all variables inline, transfer endpoint detail (value_type / transfer_type / via), and active expansion affordances. For diligence, audit, investigation.
density.rule: Levels are cumulative and add both detail and chroma. Light must be fully legible with zero color.

link.description: The atomic record. One relationship between exactly two entities.

required.source: Entity id (string), or endpoint object {id, value_type, transfer_type}.
required.destination: Entity id (string), or endpoint object {id, value_type, transfer_type}. Canonical link field; a d3-force renderer aliases it to d3's target on load (see the reference renderer buildG). target is accepted as an input synonym.
required.type: Relationship type (open string). Gives the relationship its meaning; drives edge color.

optional.variables: Qualitative and other detail (object). Revealed by density level.
optional.via: Exchange/clearing entity between source and destination (string or array). May equal source.id or destination.id for an on-its-own-book exchange. A graph member.
optional.id: Stable link identifier, when the relationship must be referenced.
optional.label: Display label. Defaults to type.

endpoint_object.id: Entity id; must resolve to a graph member (source/destination/via somewhere).
endpoint_object.value_type: What is moving on this side (USD, USDC, EUR).
endpoint_object.transfer_type: How it moves on this side (wire, ach, solana, canton). A change in value_type or transfer_type between source and destination signals an Exchange; name the exchanger in via.

node.description: An optional annotation for an entity already referenced by a link. Nodes do not create graph membership.

required.id: Entity id. Must match a source, destination, or via (or endpoint id) in at least one link. Use a stable id distinct from label when labels can collide.

optional.type: Entity category (open string). Drives node color and shape. See entity_types.
optional.label: Display label. Defaults to id.
optional.weight: Quantitative magnitude (number). Drives node size. Defaults to degree if absent.
optional.variables: Qualitative and other detail. Common: sub_type, description, status.
optional.more: Expansion disclosure: {exists, count, types}. See more.

variables.description: The extension point on links and nodes. Optional object. The type defines what the variables mean; GSF does not enforce a global ontology.
variables.value_vs_values: Keep the one quantitative magnitude in weight (drives size/width). Put qualitative values (status, category, jurisdiction, label) in variables (drive color, grouping, filtering).
rules: If present, variables must be a JSON object.
Put relationship detail in link.variables, entity detail in node.variables.
Do not invent new top-level fields for type-specific detail. Use variables.

view.description: The part of a dataset that tells the renderer what to show and how much. GSF defines these fields; a dataset sets them.

degree.means: Breadth: hops from a focus entity. Independent of level.
degree.note: Defaults only. A dataset may set any degree at any level.

focus.means: Where breadth is measured from. Empty for a full, unfocused graph.

layout.note: renderer (root field) names the engine; layout.type selects the algorithm within it. Force-directed unless overridden.
layout.params: Algorithm tuning. For force: charge, link_distance, collide_padding, alpha_decay, group_x_strength (default 0), center_y_strength (default 0).
layout.group_by: Optional node field for spatial grouping (e.g. type). Off by default; topology drives layout.

encoding.description: Optional override of the default encoding. Omit to use the canonical rules in the encoding section.
encoding.cluster_by: Optional node field for spatial grouping (sets layout.group_by).

optional.filters: Optional type filters. Filters never remove the more.exists disclosure obligation.

entity_types.description: Seven open categories for node.type, each with a canonical shape. Sub-types (e.g. country, company, wallet, value_type) may be used directly as node.type; the renderer maps known sub-types to their parent for consistent encoding. Custom types are allowed.

categories.person: An individual human. Shape: circle. e.g. founder, officer.
categories.place: A geographic or jurisdictional location. Shape: diamond. e.g. country, city. Often the Issuer in value-layer graphs.
categories.org: A formal or informal organization. Shape: rounded-rect. e.g. company, government, fund.
categories.event: A discrete occurrence shared by two or more entities. Shape: triangle. e.g. a named, dated convening - use an event node when the event is the shared context, not a variable on a link.
categories.thing: A physical or digital artifact. Shape: square. e.g. asset, document, contract.
categories.concept: An abstract category, standard, or taxonomy root. Shape: hexagon. e.g. value_type, standard. Valid when it carries real domain meaning, not as a synthetic scope anchor.
categories.system: A platform, account, wallet, rail, or network endpoint. Shape: pill. e.g. transfer_type, chain, account. Often the TransferType in value-layer graphs.

relationship_types.description: link.type is an OPEN STRING. There is no closed list - a dataset defines whatever types it needs (a real value-layer graph used founder, cofounder, operates, spinout, and program_partner, none defined here, and it rendered fine). The few conventions below exist only so common cases encode consistently; put detail in variables.

conventions.transaction: directed - value moved, same value_type and transfer_type on both sides (no conversion).
conventions.transfers_via: directed - value moved with a conversion; endpoint objects carry each side, via names the exchanger.
conventions.instruction: directed - a data/instruction/receipt/confirmation leg that carries no value (CSF's dashed [DATA] arrow).
conventions.issues: directed - an issuer (org/place) issues a value type or asset.
conventions.supports: directed - a transfer mechanism or system supports a value type or concept.
conventions.represents: directed - a derived value type is pegged to another (USDC represents USD).
conventions.part_of: directed - an entity belongs to a larger concept, system, or taxonomy.

more.description: Discloses whether a visible node has relationships outside the current view. The honesty primitive.

fields.exists: boolean - true when more relationships exist beyond what is shown.
fields.count: integer or null - number of hidden relationships; null when existence is known but count is not.
fields.types: object - optional hidden counts keyed by relationship type.

rules: Include more when the source system can determine expansion status.
If exists is true, the renderer must show an expansion indicator.
Filters and permissions do not remove the obligation: if known relationships are hidden, exists stays true.

anti_patterns.type_on_node_only: Leaving links typeless and inferring meaning from endpoint types. Use explicit link types.
anti_patterns.synthetic_scope_anchor: A hub node whose only job is to hold unrelated entities in one view. Use view.focus for scope. A concept node with real domain meaning is not this.
anti_patterns.analytics_in_the_graph: Embedding scores, centrality, or alerts as base graph fields. Run analytics downstream.
anti_patterns.relationship_as_property: Encoding a relationship as a node variable instead of a link. If two entities relate, it is a link.

validation: Root has format (gsf), version, renderer (d3-force), view, links.
view.level is light, medium, or heavy. view.degree is an integer. view.focus is an array.
Every link has source, destination, type, and connects exactly two entities.
Datasets may name the second endpoint destination (canonical) or target (synonym); a renderer treats them identically, aliasing to d3's target.
Endpoint objects use id as the entity identifier; a change in value_type/transfer_type implies an exchange.
If nodes are present, every node id appears as a source, destination, or via in some link. No orphans.
weight, if present, is a number. variables, if present, is an object.
more.exists, if present, is a boolean; more.count is an integer or null.
Analytics are not required for validity and must not be a base requirement.

llm_instructions.role: Convert source material into a GSF dataset for D3 force-directed visualization. Output the dataset (JSON), not the rules.
instructions: Output valid JSON only: {format, version, renderer, view, nodes?, links}.
Encode every relationship as a link with source, destination, and type. Never as a node property.
Nodes are optional annotations; every node id must appear in a link (source, destination, or via).
Put the one quantitative magnitude in weight (drives size/width). Put qualitative detail in variables (drives color/grouping/tooltip).
Classify nodes with the seven categories: person, place, thing, event, org, concept, system.
Relationship types are open strings; use a clear verb and define whatever the data needs.

For the value layer: Issuer (place/org) issues ValueType (concept); TransferType (system) supports ValueType; stablecoin represents fiat; represent an exchange as one transfers_via link with differing endpoint objects and a via.
Use undirected for symmetric relationships (sibling, coworker, partner, integration).
Set view.level by how much detail is wanted and view.degree by how many hops; they are independent.
Include node.more when expansion status is known; never imply completeness falsely.
Do not connect clusters with a synthetic anchor; use a real shared entity or a genuine containing concept.
Do not embed analytics, scores, or alerts unless they are source relationship data.

value_layer._comment: Issuer issues ValueType; rail supports it; stablecoin represents fiat; everything part_of the Value Layer concept. Type drives shape; no synthetic anchor.

transfer_exchange._comment: An Exchange: endpoint objects differ in value_type/transfer_type; via names the exchanger; weight is the amount.

changelog.0.5.1: Shrunk the spec ~30% toward the grammar. Trimmed the changelog to a pointer (the repo is git). Cut the relationship-type catalog to a few illustrative conventions (types are open strings; real graphs invent their own). Compressed entity_types to one line per category. Merged core_rules into a lean rules list. Removed dataset_shape (it equalled the minimal example). Moved value_layer_profile and csf_interop out to the standards README, where the GSF<->CSF relationship and the value-layer mapping live alongside both formats.
changelog.0.5.0: Restructured around four primitives (relationship, type, variables, weight); added the value-vs-values rule, the encoding section, canonical per-type shapes, and achromatic Light (color is an optional overlay from Medium).
changelog.prior: 0.1.0 - 0.4.0: see git history in the standards repo.
